title: The Macro Minute: Is Kevin Warsh a hawk or a dove?

Happy Wednesday out there team 42, it's
your skipper here Darius Dale with our
macro minute for Wednesday, April 22nd,
2026. As always, we'll start with the
executive summary from today's lead off
morning note. So, let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is, is Kevin
Warsh a hawk or a dove? Our answer is
Kevin Warsh did an excellent job in his
Senate confirmation hearing yesterday,
confirming our expectation that he is
the right man to usher in much needed
regime change at the Fed to among other
things project the Fed's broken models,
broken frameworks, its Keynesian
academic dogma, and clear left-leaning
bias to create more prosperous outcomes
for all members of society, not just us
in the established wealth class. Refer
to our October 2026 Fed op-ed for more
details on this.
That said, however, Warsh did a
historically poor job of defending his
independence from President Trump, and
by extension the Fed's independence from
political interference. Still, financial
markets repriced in a hawkish manner
amid his testimony, so there was some
hope that he was merely pandering to the
boss. Time will tell on that front. Uh
before we transition to our 42 macro
dashboard, uh the one thing I would also
say, uh we break this down in our uh
monetary policy section as well in the
in as well as in the charts on slide uh
16 and 18 from today's note, is that uh
of all the markets that repriced in a
hawkish manner yesterday, and there were
quite a quite many of them, uh one
market that didn't reprice in a hawkish
manner is the repo market. The repo
market was, you know, uh unchanged. Uh
and so, that is consistent with our view
that Kevin Warsh's hawkish views on the
balance sheet are going to be offset and
potentially overwhelmed by financial
sector deregulation and legislation that
financially represses
US commercial banks and global
stablecoin issuers uh to supporting uh
the Treasury market. Uh in our view, we
don't think that'll eventually be
enough, but in it's highly likely that
financial repression via those two uh
agents uh is the next stop on this
train. So, uh refer to the note today uh
for more details on that, and
particularly from what it looks like
from the perspective of banking sector
regulation, uh the swapping of uh bank
reserves uh for Treasuries, for banks'
high-quality liquid assets, uh etc. Uh
transition to our 42 macro dashboard as
always to wrap up uh with the question
from our community. This one's titled
straight up from Moos, non-linear supply
chain breakages. Says, I've been
re-listening to Luke Gromen, and he's
concerned about COVID-style non-linear
supply chains breaking. Right now, the
markets seem to be pricing in the
hormones open soon, and everything
smooths out quickly uh via his 6-week
helium, sulfuric fertilizer, oil gap
plus force majeures on petroleum
byproducts, uh and Russia, Kazakhstan,
China oil, diesel export bans, etc. uh
breaking things premise. Uh I know we're
relying on your VAMs uh to get us in and
out, uh but I since I joined a month
ago, I'm not sure how well it does uh
for the March 2020-style uh cliff down
moves. Uh if there's such an event
coming, and there's even more leverage
systematic flows now, might it drop even
faster or sharper and should it uh
happen in the near future. So, excellent
question. I think this is the key risk,
and this is something we've been
highlighting uh in the context of our
uh our our our our our our thoughts on
the liquidity cycle, specifically uh the
the the fact that these these products
are being embargoed is essentially
throwing a massive wrench in the global
dollar recycling cycle from the
perspective of the net international
investment surplus economies in the Gulf
Coast, as well as the net international
investment surplus economies in Asia and
Europe. If they don't get those barrels
of crude oil, if they don't get the
fertilizer inputs they need, we're going
to see a just an evaporation
[clears throat]
of global liquidity at some point uh in
the in the coming weeks if if not
months. Um you know, it's hard to to
have precision on that. But one thing we
can rely on that helps us sleep uh
comfortably at night is that KISS and
Dr. Mo are an extremely effective risk
management tools, particularly for
dealing with something like a sudden
stop risk in global financial markets.
So, just looking at our Dr. Mo signal
here for the S&P 500, we can actually go
back and zoom in on the uh on how well
Dr. Mo traded uh the the COVID collapse.
You know, the the green um the green
indicates the dark green indicates a
long max position uh in Dr. in KISS and
Dr. Mo. Uh the light green uh position
indicates a long half position, and then
the gray indicates a no position. So,
Dr. Mo would gotten us out on a rolling
out-of-sample basis uh from the S&P 500
on the 2nd of March uh in 2020, and
gotten us back in uh starting on I want
to say March 27th or or March 28th here.
Uh yeah, on March 30th on that Monday.
So, uh you know, this is exactly why our
company exists, is to help investors
chop off the left tail of the
distribution so that they can have the
confidence to remain invested so that
they can actually move towards their
strategic investment objectives, which
if you're a retail investor should be uh
to retire on time and comfortably. So,
we'll wrap it up there. Uh Darius Dale
here uh presenting our macro minute for
Wednesday, April 22nd, 2026. Best of
luck out there today. We'll catch you
back here tomorrow. Cheers.
